channel.title: Guide Japan — Your English Guide to Life in Japan on Guide Japan — Life in Japan for Foreigners
channel.description: Recent content in Guide Japan — Your English Guide to Life in Japan on Guide Japan — Life in Japan for Foreigners

item.title: Cost of Living in Tokyo 2025: Complete Monthly Breakdown
item.description:
Tokyo has a reputation for being expensive — but is it really? Compared to cities like London, New York, or Sydney, Tokyo is surprisingly affordable once you know where to look. Here’s a real monthly breakdown for expats in 2025.

Monthly Budget: Tokyo at a Glance

Expense Category Budget Comfortable Comfortable+
Rent (1K/1DK) ¥60,000–80K ¥90,000–130K ¥150,000+
Food & groceries ¥25,000 ¥40,000 ¥60,000+
Transport ¥8,000 ¥12,000 ¥20,000
Utilities ¥10,000 ¥15,000 ¥20,000
Phone plan ¥2,500 ¥4,000 ¥7,000
Health insurance ¥5,000–10K ¥15,000 ¥25,000
Entertainment ¥10,000 ¥25,000 ¥50,000+
Total estimate ¥120–150K ¥200–250K ¥330K+

In USD (at ¥150/$1): Budget ~$800–1,000/month, Comfortable ~$1,300–1,700/month.

item.title: How to Buy an Akiya (Vacant House) in Japan: Complete Guide for Foreigners
item.description:
Japan has an extraordinary opportunity hiding in plain sight: over 9 million vacant homes (akiya) scattered across the country, many available for as little as ¥1–5 million (roughly $7,000–$35,000 USD). Here’s how foreigners can buy one.

What Is an Akiya?

Akiya (空き家) literally means “empty house” in Japanese. These are properties left vacant due to Japan’s aging population, rural depopulation, and inheritance complications. Many are structurally sound homes in scenic rural areas — abandoned simply because owners moved to cities or passed away without heirs willing to maintain them.

item.title: Japan Tourist Visa Guide: Everything You Need to Know (2025)
item.description:
Japan is one of the world’s most popular travel destinations — and for good reason. But before you pack your bags, you need to understand Japan’s entry requirements. Here’s everything you need to know about Japan’s tourist visa in 2025.

Do You Need a Visa to Visit Japan?

It depends on your nationality. Japan has visa-exemption agreements with over 60 countries, meaning citizens of those countries can visit without a visa for short stays (typically 15, 30, or 90 days).
